faq.answer: The title 'Atatürk' means 'Father of the Turks' and was bestowed upon him by the Turkish parliament in 1934.

faq.question: What is Atatürk's status in the Republic of Turkey?
faq.answer: He is revered in Turkey as an independence hero and the founding father of the nation. The Turkish parliament granted him the honorific 'Atatürk', and he is the sole individual featured on Turkish banknotes.

faq.question: When did Atatürk die and what was the cause of death?
faq.answer: He died on November 10, 1938, at the age of 57. He had been diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver earlier in 1938.

faq.question: What were the major reform policies implemented by Atatürk?
faq.answer: He implemented numerous reforms, including the abolition of the Caliphate, the legislation of secularism, dress reforms, securing women's right to education, the abolition of the Islamic calendar, the ban on polygamy, the shift to the Latin alphabet, and granting women the right to vote.

timeline.event: Born as the second daughter to William Edward Nightingale and Frances Nightingale.
Meets the Swiss socialist Sismondi while traveling in Italy.
Observes a hospital in Alexandria while traveling in Egypt and realizes the importance of formal nursing education.
Studies nursing at a Protestant school in Kaiserswerth, Germany, and becomes the superintendent of a women's hospital in London.
Serves as a military nurse at a field hospital in Scutari during the Crimean War.
Elected as the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.
Establishes the Nightingale Training School for Nurses and authors professional nursing texts.
Awarded the Dame of Grace of the Order of St John (DStJ).
Becomes the first woman to receive the Order of Merit from King Edward VII.
Passes away at the age of 90.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Nursing Profession
keyAchievements.description: By serving as a military nurse in the Crimean War, she popularized the profession of nursing and matured it into a specialized field.

keyAchievements.title: Improving Hospital Hygiene
keyAchievements.description: Implemented discipline and sanitary conditions in the chaotic field hospital at Scutari during the Crimean War, reducing the mortality rate from 42 percent to 2 percent.

keyAchievements.title: Building a Nursing Education System
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for nursing education by establishing the Nightingale Training School for Nurses in 1860 and authoring professional nursing texts.

keyAchievements.title: Proposing the Concept of Intensive Care Units
keyAchievements.description: First proposed the concept of an intensive care unit during the Crimean War, suggesting that critically ill patients be isolated for specialized care.

keyAchievements.title: Statistical Analysis
keyAchievements.description: Used data analysis from the Crimean War to argue for the importance of hospital hygiene, becoming the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Her book 'Notes on Nursing' served as the foundational text for nursing practice and education, and the Nightingale Pledge is still recited by nurses worldwide.

faq.question: Have there been any critical views of the veneration of Nightingale?
faq.answer: In 1999, the British public sector union UNISON opposed the veneration of Nightingale, arguing that it hindered nursing independence and contributed to low wages, while also overshadowing the achievements of other nurses.

faq.question: Were there other notable nurses active during Nightingale's time?
faq.answer: Mary Seacole from Jamaica was active during the same period, but due to racial discrimination, she was unable to work alongside Nightingale and lived in poverty.

timeline.event: Born as the second of three sons and one daughter to Yu Jung-gwon and Lee So-je in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan, South Chungcheong Province.
Transferred to the 3rd year of the common course at Ewha Haktang as a scholarship student upon the recommendation of Alice Sharp, an American Methodist missionary.
Graduated from the common course at Ewha Haktang and enrolled in the higher course.
Participated in the March 1st Movement in Seoul.
Participated in the Manse (independence) demonstrations in Seoul.
Returned to her hometown of Cheonan with her cousin Yu Ye-do after Ewha Haktang was closed by order of the Governor-General.
Arrested by the Japanese while playing a leading role in the Manse demonstration at the Aunae Market in Cheonan.
Sentenced to 5 years in prison by the Gongju District Court for sedition and violation of the Security Law.
Sentenced to 3 years in prison by the Gyeongseong Appeals Court; the sentence was confirmed after she abandoned her appeal.
Died in Seodaemun Prison due to a ruptured bladder.
Following protests by Ewha Haktang staff, her body was received from Seodaemun Prison and dressed in a shroud.
A funeral service was held at Jeongdong Methodist Church, and she was buried in the Itaewon public cemetery.
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independence Medal.
Because her remains were lost, a cenotaph (Chohonmyo) was erected halfway up Maebongsan Mountain in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan.
Her birthplace in Yongdu-ri, Byeongcheon-myeon, Dongnam-gu, Cheonan City, South Chungcheong Province, was restored and designated as Historic Site No. 230.
Ewha Girls' High School awarded her an honorary diploma.
The Yu Gwan-sun Memorial Hall opened in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan, South Chungcheong Province, to mark the 100th anniversary of her birth.
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Republic of Korea Medal.

keyAchievements.title: Participation and Leadership in the March 1st Movement
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the March 1st Movement in Seoul in 1919 and, upon returning to her hometown of Cheonan, took a leading role in organizing the Manse demonstration at the Aunae Market.

keyAchievements.title: Martyrdom for the Independence Movement
keyAchievements.description: Arrested by the Japanese and died a martyr in Seodaemun Prison from a ruptured bladder caused by severe torture, demonstrating an unwavering commitment to the independence movement.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of the Spirit of Independence
keyAchievements.description: Remembered as a symbolic figure of the independence movement, she was posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation (Independence Medal and Republic of Korea Medal), and memorial projects have been established in her honor.

faq.question: What is Yu Gwan-sun's family clan (bon-gwan)?
faq.answer: Yu Gwan-sun's family clan is Goheung.

faq.question: Was there any controversy regarding the spelling of her name?
faq.answer: There was past debate over whether to spell her surname as 'Yu' or 'Ryu' due to the initial sound rule in Korean. Since 2014, it has been officially spelled as 'Yu Gwan-sun' to reduce public confusion.

faq.question: What was Yu Gwan-sun's sentence in the first trial?
faq.answer: On May 9, 1919, the Gongju District Court sentenced her to 5 years in prison for sedition and violating the Security Law.

faq.question: What was the cause of Yu Gwan-sun's death?
faq.answer: She died in prison on September 28, 1920, from a ruptured bladder caused by the torture she endured while serving her sentence in Seodaemun Prison.

faq.question: Where are Yu Gwan-sun's remains?
faq.answer: Her remains were lost during the relocation of the Itaewon public cemetery. Currently, there is a cenotaph (Chohonmyo) erected halfway up Maebongsan Mountain in her hometown of Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan.

faq.question: How did Yu Gwan-sun's parents die?
faq.answer: Her father, Yu Jung-gwon, and mother, Lee So-je, were killed on the spot by the Japanese during the Aunae Manse demonstration on April 1, 1919.

timeline.event: Born as Siddhartha Gautama in Lumbini (modern-day Nepal) to a royal family.
Renounced his princely life, abandoning his family and kingdom to seek spiritual enlightenment.
Attained supreme enlightenment under the Bodhi tree in Bodh Gaya.
Delivered his first sermon at the Deer Park in Sarnath, setting the Wheel of Dharma in motion.
Passed away (attained Mahaparinirvana) in Kushinagar at the age of 80.
Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland, to Isaac Rousseau, a watchmaker, and Suzanne Bernard.
His mother, Suzanne Bernard, died from complications of childbirth.
His father moved due to a dispute with the city of Geneva, and Rousseau entered a boarding school near Geneva.
Began receiving vocational training to become a court clerk.
Received Roman Catholic baptism in Turin, Italy, and was given the baptismal name Jean-Joseph.
Moved to Lausanne and lived as a music teacher under a pseudonym.
Visited Paris for the first time, then lived in places like Chambéry via Lyon.
Lived near Madame de Warens in Chambéry and Les Charmettes, devoting himself to music and reading to build his education.
Met and formed friendships with the Enlightenment figures d'Alembert and Diderot in Paris.
Organized a new system of musical notation and presented it at the French Academy, but it was not successful.
After 'Dissertation sur la musique moderne' was published, he moved to Venice to serve as secretary to the Comte de Montaigu.
Married the maid Marie-Thérèse Levasseur after a long period of cohabitation.
Made a name for himself when his 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences' won first prize in an academic essay contest held by the Academy of Dijon, and his opera 'Le Devin du village' was performed.
Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy, and published the 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences'.
Composed the opera 'Le Devin du village'.
Immersed himself in political and social institutions for an essay contest by the Academy of Dijon, forming the basis for 'The Social Contract'.
Settled at the Hermitage and began writing 'The Social Contract', 'Julie, or the New Heloise', and 'Emile'.
Had a disagreement with Diderot over the social function of theater and broke off their friendship a year later.
'Julie, or the New Heloise' was published in Paris and achieved great success.
Published 'Emile' and 'The Social Contract', which led to persecution and his fleeing to Switzerland.
Began writing 'Confessions' after being attacked by Voltaire's anonymous work 'The Sentiment of the Citizens'.
Completed 'Confessions'.
Returned to Paris.
Wrote 'Rousseau Judge of Jean-Jacques'.
Began writing 'Reveries of the Solitary Walker' but failed to finish it.
Died on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: The Social Contract
keyAchievements.description: A book that perfected the theory of popular sovereignty and established the general will as the condition justifying the exercise of power.

keyAchievements.title: Discourse on the Arts and Sciences
keyAchievements.description: The first-prize winning essay at the Academy of Dijon, containing ideas that go beyond the limits of the Enlightenment.

keyAchievements.title: Discourse on the Origin of Inequality
keyAchievements.description: A work that explains the inequality arising from the development of property systems and social organizations by contrasting it with the state of nature.

keyAchievements.title: Emile
keyAchievements.description: Described a theory of natural religion that greatly influenced 18th-century intellectual circles, though it faced persecution.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Compilation of the Encyclopédie
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the General Will
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the concept of human nature's will for a form of association seeking the common good and equal liberty.

keyAchievements.title: Radical Egalitarian Ideas
keyAchievements.description: Delved into the issue of human equality practically for the first time in history, and asserted that economic inequality is the root of all social evils.

faq.question: Who was Jean-Jacques Rousseau?
faq.answer: Jean-Jacques Rousseau was a French social contract theorist, direct democrat, republican, and Enlightenment philosopher born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland.

faq.question: What does Rousseau's 'general will' mean?
faq.answer: Rousseau's general will is the will to seek a form of association that defends and protects the person and goods of each associate with the whole common force, and in which each, while uniting himself with all, may still obey himself alone, and remain as free as before.

faq.question: Why did Rousseau argue to 'return to nature'?
faq.answer: Rousseau did not reject civilization itself, but rather criticized the absurdities and contradictions of a civilized society that lacked freedom and equality, and strongly advocated restoring the archetype of a society that guarantees human's natural rights of freedom and equality.

faq.question: What are Rousseau's major works?
faq.answer: Rousseau's major works include 'The Social Contract', 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences', 'Discourse on the Origin of Inequality', 'Emile', and 'Julie, or the New Heloise'.

timeline.event: Born in Switzerland.
His father, Paul Jung, was appointed to a larger parish in Laufen (when Jung was 6).
Met Sigmund Freud for the first time in Vienna.
Began developing his analytical psychology theories independently.
Resigned from the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Served as president of the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Published the book 'Psychology of the Unconscious'.
Published the book 'A Psychological Approach to the Dogma of the Trinity'.
Published the book 'Transformation Symbolism in the Mass'.
Published the book 'Answer to Job'.
Passed away at the age of 85.
The book 'Memories, Dreams, Reflections' was published (posthumous publication).

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytical Psychology
keyAchievements.description: As a Swiss psychiatrist, he pursued an independent path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of Complex
keyAchievements.description: Used the word 'complex' to describe specific states and laid the foundation for the related theories.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of the Collective Unconscious
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the collective unconscious, a repository of mental materials that are part of the personal unconscious but shared through the history and culture of humanity rather than personal experience.

keyAchievements.title: Personality Classification (Introversion/Extraversion)
keyAchievements.description: Classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the Concept of Individuation
keyAchievements.description: Developed and theorized psychotherapy, viewing that individuals undergoing psychotherapy could advance into whole persons through the process of 'individuation'.

keyAchievements.title: Asserted the Self Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Asserted that the self archetype, which allows the unconscious within humans to realize a unified whole, possesses a transcendent function.

keyAchievements.title: Concepts of Anima and Animus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concepts of anima and animus, which represent the opposite gender in a person's psyche.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the archetype, a term borrowed from anthropology meaning universal human mental images or themes.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Persona
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of persona, an aspect of personality that appears for adaptation or personal convenience.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Shadow
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the shadow, which includes repressed and poorly known aspects of the personality often considered negative.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Self
keyAchievements.description: Viewed the 'self', a central concept governing the individuation process and symbolized by a mandala that unifies the feminine and masculine, as the main archetype of the psyche.

faq.question: Who is Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung was a Swiss psychiatrist, the founder of analytical psychology, who established the concepts of the complex and collective unconscious, and classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

faq.question: Why did Carl Jung and Freud part ways?
faq.answer: Freud restricted the concept of 'libido' to sexuality, while Jung saw it more broadly as general energy, which led to a divergence of views and their split.

faq.question: What is the field of psychology founded by Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung pursued his own path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

faq.question: How did Carl Jung speak about God?
faq.answer: He mentioned about God in a broadcast interview, saying, "I do not believe in Him, I know Him."

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Born in Woolsthorpe, Lincolnshire, England; his father, Isaac Newton Sr., died in October 1642, before he was born.
When Newton was three, his mother, Hannah Ayscough, remarried, and he was left in the care of his maternal grandmother.
His mother returned after his stepfather died.
Sent to The King's School in Grantham to begin classical education; boarded with an apothecary named Mr. Clark, where he showed an interest in chemistry.
His mother called him back to Woolsthorpe to teach him farming.
Admitted to Trinity College, Cambridge.
Generalized the binomial theorem; when Cambridge University closed due to the Great Plague, he returned home for two years.
Returned to Cambridge University when it reopened and earned his Master's degree.
Built the reflecting telescope.
Succeeded Professor Isaac Barrow as Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge University and began his research on calculus.
Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and developed his theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.
Discovered the interference phenomenon in thin films.
Published Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica, presenting the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and proving the three laws of motion.
Served as a Member of Parliament.
Served as the Master of the Royal Mint.
Became President of the Royal Society of London following the death of Robert Hooke.
Knighted by Queen Anne.
Devised an experiment to verify the existence of the aether.
Died in his sleep at the age of 84, and his remains were interred in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, and created Newton's method for finding the roots of a function.

keyAchievements.title: Generalization of the Binomial Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the generalized binomial theorem and contributed to the study of power series.

keyAchievements.title: Classical Mechanics and Universal Gravitation
keyAchievements.description: Provided the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation and formulated Newton's laws of motion in Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Reflecting Telescope
keyAchievements.description: Built the first practical reflecting telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Light
keyAchievements.description: Developed the theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.

keyAchievements.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
keyAchievements.description: Formulated Newton's law of cooling through experiments, studied the speed of sound, and introduced the concept of a Newtonian fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Inverse-Square Law of Gravity
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the inverse-square law of gravity, which states that the gravitational attraction between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

faq.question: Who was Isaac Newton?
faq.answer: Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer.

faq.question: What is Newton's most influential book?
faq.answer: Published in 1687, Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica established the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and is considered one of the most influential books in the history of science.

faq.question: How did Newton contribute to calculus?
faq.answer: He contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and discovered Newton's method for approximating the roots of a function.

faq.question: What are Newton's laws of motion?
faq.answer: Newton's laws of motion consist of three laws: the law of inertia, the law of acceleration, and the law of action and reaction.

faq.question: How did Newton explain gravity?
faq.answer: He revealed the inverse-square law of gravity and explained the law of universal gravitation, showing that the force that pulls an apple to the ground is the same force that keeps the moon in its orbit.

faq.question: Did Newton ever marry?
faq.answer: He was engaged in his youth, but he was so devoted to his research and work that it did not lead to marriage, and he remained single his entire life.

faq.question: What were Newton's religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He rejected the Trinity but was deeply religious; he believed in a single Creator and reportedly spent a great deal of time on biblical interpretation.

faq.question: What public offices did Newton hold?
faq.answer: He held public roles as a Member of Parliament, Master of the Royal Mint, and President of the Royal Society of London.

timeline.event: Galileo di Vincenzo Bonaiuti de' Galilei is born in Pisa, Tuscany, Italy.
Becomes a professor of mathematics at the University of Pisa.
Writes On Motion (De Motu).
Improves upon a 3x telescope invented in the Netherlands and demonstrates his first telescope to Venetian lawmakers.
Discovers the four largest moons of Jupiter using a telescope and publishes Sidereus Nuncius (Starry Messenger).
Accepts the invitation of Cosimo II, Grand Duke of Tuscany, moves to Florence, and continues his research.
Visits Rome and becomes a member of the Accademia dei Lincei.
Ordered by Cardinal Robert Bellarmine not to defend or teach Copernican astronomy.
Publishes The Assayer, attacking theories based on Aristotelian authority and advancing a mathematical system of scientific thought.
Publishes Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems, which leads to his trial by the Roman Inquisition.
Found guilty by the Inquisition, placed under house arrest, and all his works are placed on the Index of Forbidden Books.
Completes Discourses and Mathematical Demonstrations Relating to Two New Sciences, and becomes completely blind.
Galileo dies.
Pope John Paul II acknowledges that the Galileo trial was unjust and apologizes.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the Telescope and Astronomical Observations
keyAchievements.description: Improved the telescope and discovered Jupiter's moons, lunar craters, and sunspots; his observation of the phases of Venus supported the Copernican heliocentric theory.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Laws of Motion
keyAchievements.description: Studied uniformly accelerated motion to establish kinematics, and established the concepts of velocity and acceleration, which laid the foundation for Newton's laws of motion.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Scientific Method
keyAchievements.description: Established universal mathematical laws and the quantitative analysis of empirical facts, leading to the beginning of physics in the modern sense.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Jupiter's Four Largest Moons
keyAchievements.description: Discovered Io, Europa, Callisto, and Ganymede, proving that not all celestial bodies revolve around the Earth, which contradicted the Aristotelian worldview.

faq.question: Did Galileo Galilei actually perform the drop experiment from the Leaning Tower of Pisa?
faq.answer: No, this experiment was a story invented by Viviani, Galileo's biographer and disciple, to elevate him; Galileo refuted Aristotle's theory through logical reasoning.

faq.question: Was Galileo a devout Catholic despite his conflicts with the Church?
faq.answer: Yes, despite his conflict with the Church, he was a devout Roman Catholic, to the point where he once hoped to become a Catholic priest.

faq.question: Is the anecdote true that Galileo muttered 'And yet it moves' after his Inquisition trial?
faq.answer: No, while this widely known anecdote illustrates his passion for seeking scientific truth, there is no reliable evidence that Galileo actually said it.

faq.question: What stance did the Vatican take on Galileo's trial concerning heliocentrism?
faq.answer: In 1992, Pope John Paul II acknowledged the injustice of the Galileo trial and issued an apology; earlier, in 1744, Galileo's book 'Dialogue' was removed from the Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

timeline.event: Claude Monet is born on November 14 in the 9th arrondissement of Paris.
Baptized Oscar-Claude on May 20 at the Notre-Dame de Lorette.
His family moves to Le Havre in Normandy.
Enrolls in the Le Havre secondary school of the arts on April 1.
His mother dies.
Meets fellow painter Eugène Boudin.
Arrives in Paris in April and settles at the Hotel 'du Nouveau Monde'.
Enrolls in the Académie Suisse, run by Charles Suisse, early in the year (around February) and meets Camille Pissarro.
Drafted into the army in Le Havre on March 2 and joins the First Regiment of African Light Cavalry on April 29.
Contracts typhoid fever in the spring, returns to Le Havre to recover, and meets Johan Jongkind in the summer.
Returns to Paris in December, enrolls in the studio of Charles Gleyre, and meets Auguste Renoir and Frédéric Bazille.
Successfully debuts at the Salon with The Point of la Hève at Low Tide and Mouth of the Seine at Honfleur.
Submits The Woman in the Green Dress and Pavé de Chailly to the Salon and is accepted.
His then-mistress Camille Doncieux gives birth to their first son, Jean.
Marries Camille on June 28, and takes refuge in London and the Netherlands just before the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
His father dies, and the family moves to Argenteuil.
Moves to the 'pink house with green shutters' in Argenteuil, and exhibits Impression, Sunrise at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
Paints Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son.
Camille Monet falls seriously ill; another exhibition is organized in opposition to the Salon.
Exhibits seven paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare at the Third Impressionist Exhibition on April 5.
His second son Michel is born, the family moves to the village of Vétheuil, and Camille is diagnosed with uterine cancer.
Camille dies.
Submits two paintings to the Salon, and one is accepted.
Moves to Poissy in December with Alice Hoschedé, the wife of Ernest Hoschedé, and her children.
Participates in his last, the Seventh Impressionist Exhibition.
Moves to a rented house in Giverny with the children on April 29, and his first major retrospective is held.
Goes on a short painting trip to the Italian Riviera and Genoa with Auguste Renoir in December.
Stays in Bordighera from January 18 to April 5, producing 38 paintings.
Travels to the Netherlands to paint tulips.
Meets John Singer Sargent in the summer.
Purchases the house in Giverny.
15 paintings of the Haystacks series are exhibited at the Galerie Durand-Ruel.
Paints 26 views of Rouen Cathedral.
Exhibits 20 paintings of Rouen Cathedral.
61 paintings are exhibited at the Galerie Georges Petit.
Begins painting water lilies, and stays in London for six weeks.
Exhibits the first garden paintings, primarily focusing on the Japanese bridge.
Expands the pond at his home; in February, an exhibition of the Water Lilies series in New York City achieves great success.
Increases the size of the water garden to nearly 4,000 square meters.
The series 'Views of the Thames in London - 1900-1904' is exhibited in May and June.
Spends the autumn at the Palazzo Barbaro in Venice with Alice.
An exhibition titled 'Water Lilies' opens on May 6, featuring 48 paintings.
His second wife Alice dies.
Travels to London to consult with the German ophthalmologist Richard Liebreich.
His eldest son Jean dies.
Begins work on a series of decorative paintings for the French government.
Begins a series of landscape paintings 'at full strength'.
Prescription for mydriatic drops temporarily alleviates his vision problems.
Undergoes cataract surgery.
With improved vision, begins retouching some of the works produced before surgery.
Dies of lung cancer at the age of 86 on December 5 and is buried in the Giverny church cemetery.
27 panel paintings are exhibited at the Musée de l'Orangerie in May.
André Masson's essay helps shift the perception of Monet's paintings.
Water Lily paintings held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York are destroyed in a fire.
Monet's house, garden, and water lily pond are bequeathed by Michel to the French Academy of Fine Arts.
Monet's garden is restored and opened to the public.
A Google Doodle is created on November 14 to commemorate Claude Monet's 161st birthday.
The Houses of Parliament, Sunlight Effect in the Fog is sold for $20.1 million.
A paper is published in the Proceedings of the Royal Society identifying the locations where the London paintings were created.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is stolen for the second time in August.
The Railway Bridge at Argenteuil is sold for $41.4 million on May 6.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is recovered in June.
Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas is sold for £40,921,250 (approx. $80,451,178) on June 24.
The Church at Vétheuil and Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas become the subject of a court case in New York in October.
A Monet painting formerly owned by a Jewish retailer is discovered in the suitcase of Cornelius Gurlitt.
The Nazi-looted artwork On the Seashore is restituted to the granddaughters of the Parlagi couple.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Pioneering Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'father of Impressionism', he is considered a founder and pioneer of the movement and a key precursor to Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Term Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Consistent Practice of En Plein Air Landscape Painting
keyAchievements.description: As the most consistent practitioner of the Impressionist philosophy over a long career, he expressed his perception of nature in plein air landscape paintings.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Series Method
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the series method of painting the same scene many times to capture changes in light and the passing of seasons, with major works including the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and Water Lilies series.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Giverny Garden
keyAchievements.description: Purchased a house and land in Giverny, northern France, and began a vast landscaping project including a water lily pond, which became the greatest source of inspiration for his art.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration for 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

faq.question: Who was Claude Monet?
faq.answer: Claude Monet was a French Impressionist painter, a founder and pioneer of the Impressionist movement, often referred to as the 'father of Impressionism'.

faq.question: Where did the term 'Impressionism' originate?
faq.answer: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

faq.question: What are Monet's representative series paintings?
faq.answer: His representative series include the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and the Water Lilies, which captivated him for the last 20 years of his life.

faq.question: Where was Monet primarily active?
faq.answer: He lived in Giverny, northern France, from 1883, where he purchased a house and land and began a vast landscaping project that included his water lily pond.

faq.question: How did Monet's eyesight affect his work?
faq.answer: Cataracts made color perception increasingly difficult, leading his paintings to become broader and more abstract; after surgery, his paintings tended to have stronger blue tones than before.

faq.question: How did Monet's work influence modern art?
faq.answer: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration to 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) was born.
Debuted in the literary world with "The Celebrated Jumping Frog".
Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) died.

keyAchievements.title: American Author
keyAchievements.description: An American author more famously known by his pen name Mark Twain.

keyAchievements.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
keyAchievements.description: A children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

keyAchievements.title: The Celebrated Jumping Frog
keyAchievements.description: The work that made him debut in the literary world in 1865.

keyAchievements.title: The Innocents Abroad
keyAchievements.description: A work that gained popularity.

keyAchievements.title: Mississippi Trilogy
keyAchievements.description: Consists of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, Life on the Mississippi, and The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, expressing a hymn to the American and free spirit.

keyAchievements.title: Social Satires
keyAchievements.description: Works set in medieval feudal Europe, such as A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court, The Prince and the Pauper, and The Mysterious Stranger.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Christian Fundamentalism
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he published Letters from the Earth, criticizing Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Slavery
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he strove to expose and rectify the contradictions of a "free country with slavery."

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's real name?
faq.answer: Mark Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens.

faq.question: What is Mark Twain's representative work?
faq.answer: His major works include The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, a children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

faq.question: Where did Mark Twain's pen name come from?
faq.answer: He used the unit of water depth, which he used during his time as a steamboat pilot on the Mississippi River, as his pen name.

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's religious stance?
faq.answer: Mark Twain was a progressive Presbyterian, but he criticized Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

timeline.event: Born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Lisbon earthquake.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Seven Years' War.
His romance with Gretchen was broken off.
Had a romance with Anna Katharina Schönkopf while studying at Leipzig University.
Wrote his first play, Die Laune des Verliebten (The Lover's Caprice).
Fostered pious religious feelings through his association with Susanne von Klettenberg while recovering at home.
Obtained a doctorate in law from the University of Strasbourg, reunited with Herder to open his eyes to the essence of literature, and laid the groundwork for the Sturm und Drang movement.
Opened a law practice.
Went to the Imperial Chamber Court in Wetzlar for legal training, where he met Charlotte Buff.
Published the play Götz von Berlichingen.
Published the novel The Sorrows of Young Werther.
Became engaged to Lili Schönemann, but broke it off shortly after.
Arrived in Weimar at the invitation of Duke Karl August of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach.
Handled state affairs for about 10 years in the early Weimar period, building a record of achievements as a privy councillor, senior privy councillor, and head of the cabinet.
Wrote the tragedy Iphigenia in Tauris (prose version).
Wrote Torquato Tasso.
Stated, "I am not an anti-Christian or an un-Christian, but a non-Christian."
Established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy, and wrote Iphigenia in Tauris (verse version).
Embarked on a journey to Italy lasting a year and a half.
Published Egmont.
Returned to Germany.
Began living with Christiane von Vulpius.
Had his eldest son, August.
Torquato Tasso was first published.
Followed Duke August in the War of the First Coalition and participated in the Battle of Valmy.
Participated in the Siege of Mainz.
Reunited with Schiller on his way back from a natural science conference in Jena and began their friendship.
Wrote the Bildungsroman Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship.
Wrote the epic poem Hermann and Dorothea.
Fell into great shock due to Schiller's death.
Completed Part I of Faust.
Completed Theory of Colours.
Wrote Italian Journey.
Wrote West-östlicher Divan.
Completed Wilhelm Meister's Journeyman Years.
Wrote the Marienbad Elegy.
Wrote Second Stay in Rome.
Completed Part II of Faust.
Passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: He completed German classical literature by creating numerous literary works such as The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

keyAchievements.title: Fostering Weimar as a Cultural Center
keyAchievements.description: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Administrator and Scientist
keyAchievements.description: He participated in state affairs serving as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar, and left vast achievements showing interest in various natural science fields such as botany, anatomy, mineralogy, geology, and the theory of colors.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing German Classical Literature
keyAchievements.description: He established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy and met Schiller, blossoming German Weimar Classicism together.

keyAchievements.title: Court Theatre Director
keyAchievements.description: As the director of the court theatre, he was active across the board in management, directing, and actor training, elevating the theatre to a world-class level.

faq.question: Who was Goethe?
faq.answer: He was a German classical writer, philosopher, and scientist, who also served as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar.

faq.question: What are Goethe's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

faq.question: What was Goethe's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Born into a Lutheran family, he developed doubts about his faith due to the Lisbon earthquake and the Seven Years' War, and referred to himself as a "non-Christian".

faq.question: What role did Goethe play in Weimar?
faq.answer: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center and participated in state affairs as an administrator.

timeline.event: Born at York House near the Strand in London.
Entered Trinity College, Cambridge.
Started his legal career by enrolling at Gray's Inn.
Returned home after his father's death.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Bossiney in Cornwall.
Became an outer barrister.
Became a bencher, a senior member of the Inn.
Elected as a Reader, a law lecturer of the Inn.
Became a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Acted as a secret advisor to Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex.
Wrote Observations on a Libel, refuting the Jesuit Robert Parsons.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Middlesex.
Became Queen's Counsel after being named legal advisor to Elizabeth I.
Arrested for failing to pay off debts.
Participated in the interrogation of the Earl of Essex for treason.
Received a knighthood as a Knight Bachelor.
Married Alice Barnham, daughter of a London alderman.
Published The Advancement of Learning.
Took the post of Solicitor General.
Officially served as a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Became Attorney General.
Prosecuted Robert Carr, Earl of Somerset, and his wife Frances Howard for murder, leading to a guilty verdict.
Sat as a regent for a month.
Appointed Lord Chancellor and became Baron Verulam.
Published Novum Organum.
Elevated to Viscount St Albans.
Impeached by Parliament on charges of bribery and stripped of all public offices and titles.
Died of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Empiricism and Laying the Groundwork for the Scientific Revolution
keyAchievements.description: He researched natural philosophy and led the development of the scientific method, laying the groundwork for the later Scientific Revolution, and is evaluated alongside Descartes as a pioneer of modern philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Advocating Empiricist Philosophy and Writing Novum Organum
keyAchievements.description: He argued for empiricism, stating that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and wrote Novum Organum, which criticized Aristotle's methodology and advocated empiricism.

keyAchievements.title: Publishing The Advancement of Learning
keyAchievements.description: In The Advancement of Learning, he classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation for future pursuits of truth.

keyAchievements.title: Famous Maxim 'Knowledge is Power'
keyAchievements.description: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

keyAchievements.title: Proposing a Library Classification System
keyAchievements.description: He patronized libraries and proposed a classification system dividing books into three categories: history, poetry, and philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Act of Union
keyAchievements.description: Arguing for the annexation of Scotland, he was evaluated as a pioneer of the Act of Union that later laid the foundation for the creation of the Kingdom of Great Britain.

faq.question: How is Francis Bacon evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as the founder of modern empiricism and, alongside Descartes, a pioneer of modern philosophy, leading the development of the scientific method to lay the groundwork for the Scientific Revolution.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's representative philosophical claims?
faq.answer: He advocated empiricism, claiming that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and proposed a method of arriving at truth through careful observation and experimentation by eliminating preconceptions.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include Novum Organum, which advocated empiricism, and The Advancement of Learning, which classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation.

faq.question: What famous quote did Francis Bacon leave?
faq.answer: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

faq.question: What public offices did Francis Bacon hold?
faq.answer: He served as Attorney General and Lord Chancellor of England during the reign of James I, and at that time, the Lord Chancellor doubled as the Prime Minister.

timeline.event: Born (estimated)
Died (estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Development of Confucian Thought
keyAchievements.description: Developed Confucius's thought and greatly influenced the transmission of Confucianism to future generations.

keyAchievements.title: The Book "Mencius"
keyAchievements.description: The book "Mencius" contains Mencius's thoughts, and it is believed to have been compiled by his disciples.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Innate Human Goodness
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the theory of innate human goodness, arguing that human nature is inherently good and that the human mind is equipped with the "Four Beginnings" of the four virtues: benevolence, righteousness, propriety, and wisdom.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Benevolent Government and Royal Politics
keyAchievements.description: After the age of 40, he traveled across the world advocating benevolent government and royal politics.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Four Beginnings
keyAchievements.description: Explained that the human mind contains the Four Beginnings: a feeling of compassion, a feeling of shame and dislike, a feeling of deference and compliance, and a feeling of right and wrong.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Five Relationships
keyAchievements.description: Presented the Theory of the Five Relationships, organizing five human relationships: intimacy between father and son, righteousness between ruler and minister, distinction between husband and wife, order between elder and junior, and trust between friends.

keyAchievements.title: Confirmation of the Right of Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed the right of revolution, stating that a monarch who betrays the will of the people and violates benevolence and righteousness is nothing but an ordinary fellow, and subduing such a monarch is not an act of regicide.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Cyclical View of History
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to prove the legitimacy of Confucius based on the cyclical view of history that a sage appears every 500 years.

faq.question: Is the story of Mencius's mother moving three times true?
faq.answer: The story of Mencius's mother moving three times is an anecdote from the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" written by the Han Dynasty scholar Liu Xiang, and the general consensus is that it is hard to view it as a historical fact.

faq.question: From whom did Mencius learn?
faq.answer: Mencius studied the Six Classics under the tutelage of Zisi, the grandson of Confucius.

faq.question: What was the name of Mencius's mother?
faq.answer: The name of Mencius's mother is Ji (伋).

faq.question: Who compiled the book "Mencius"?
faq.answer: There is a theory that the book "Mencius" was written in his later years, but it is more accurate to view it as having been compiled by his disciples.

timeline.event: Period of living as the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha
Possibility of birth
Possibility of accession to the throne
Conquest of the Kalinga region
Record of visiting Sambodhi (the sacred Bodhi tree of Bodh Gaya)
Became closer to the Buddhist Sangha and toured the empire
Began inscribing edicts to spread Dharma
Established the position of Dhamma-Mahamatra
Commissioned the expansion of the Stupa of Buddha Kanakamuni
Donated the Khalatika Cave

keyAchievements.title: Ruling the Empire
keyAchievements.description: As the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha, he ruled an empire that covered the largest part of the Indian subcontinent.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage and Spread of Buddhism
keyAchievements.description: As a patron of Buddhism, he played a crucial role in spreading Buddhism throughout ancient Asia.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Dhamma
keyAchievements.description: After the conquest of Kalinga, he dedicated himself to the spread of 'Dhamma', or righteous behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Construction and Support of Buddhist Facilities
keyAchievements.description: Buddhist legends praise him for building a large number of stupas, patronizing the Third Buddhist Council, supporting Buddhist missionaries, and making generous donations to the Sangha.

keyAchievements.title: Historical Stature in India
keyAchievements.description: Since the decipherment of the Brahmi script in the 19th century, he has been renowned as one of India's greatest emperors.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Modern India
keyAchievements.description: The emblem of the Republic of India is an adaptation of the Lion Capital of Ashoka, and the Ashoka Chakra, the wheel of Ashoka, is depicted in the center of the Indian flag.

keyAchievements.title: Social Welfare Activities
keyAchievements.description: Carried out social welfare activities such as establishing medical facilities for humans and animals, cultivating medicinal herbs, digging wells, and planting trees along roads.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of Animal Welfare
keyAchievements.description: Promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, and abolishing royal hunts.

faq.question: What does Ashoka's name mean?
faq.answer: 'A-shoka' literally means 'without sorrow'.

faq.question: What were Ashoka's main titles?
faq.answer: His main titles were 'Priyadasi' ('he who regards amiably' or 'of gracious mien') and 'Devanampiya' ('beloved of the gods').

faq.question: Was Ashoka a Buddhist?
faq.answer: In his Minor Rock Edict 1, he clearly identified himself as a Buddhist by calling himself an upasaka (Buddhist lay follower) and a Shakya (Buddhist).

faq.question: Did Ashoka persecute other religions?
faq.answer: His edicts emphasize tolerance for all sects, donating caves to non-Buddhist ascetics and stating that both Brahmins and Shramanas deserve respect.

faq.question: What did 'Dhamma' mean to Ashoka?
faq.answer: To Ashoka, Dhamma meant a moral policy encompassing active social concern, religious tolerance, ecological awareness, observance of common ethical precepts, and the renunciation of war.

faq.question: Was Ashoka a violent person before his conversion to Buddhism?
faq.answer: Both the Sri Lankan and North Indian traditions claim that Ashoka was a violent person before embracing Buddhism.

faq.question: How did the Kalinga War affect Ashoka?
faq.answer: The destruction that occurred during the war evoked remorse for violence in him, and in the years that followed, he was drawn to Buddhism.

faq.question: What efforts did Ashoka make for animal welfare?
faq.answer: He promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, abolishing royal hunts, and establishing animal hospitals.

timeline.event: Born during the Tang Dynasty era.
In his hometown of Shu, he practiced Daoism while climbing the mountains of Sichuan with a hermit named Dongyanzi, and later left his hometown to travel around Jiangnan.
At the age of 43, he received an edict from Emperor Xuanzong and stayed in Chang'an as a Hanlin Gongfeng (academician).
Feeling bored with his life as an official, he argued with Gao Lishi, a close aide to Emperor Xuanzong, and resigned.
Returned to Jiangnan.
Joined the army of Prince Yong Lin, the sixteenth son of Emperor Xuanzong, but Prince Yong's army was designated as a rebel force, leading to his exile to Yelang (present-day Guizhou).
Died at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

keyAchievements.title: Great Poet in Chinese History
keyAchievements.description: A poet of the Tang Dynasty era, he is considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history along with Du Fu.

keyAchievements.title: Poet Immortal (Shixian)
keyAchievements.description: Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal', and his poetic talent was evaluated as 'genius'.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet
keyAchievements.description: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive, and his poetic literature themed around Daoism and wine is famous.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Gushi (Ancient Style Poetry) and Jueju (Quatrains)
keyAchievements.description: He specialized in gushi and jueju, and his jueju were evaluated as 'divine works'.

keyAchievements.title: Free and Fantastic Poetic Style
keyAchievements.description: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

faq.question: What were Li Bai's courtesy name and pseudonym?
faq.answer: Li Bai's courtesy name was Taibai, and his pseudonym was Qinglian Jushi (Householder of the Azure Lotus).

faq.question: By what nickname was Li Bai called?
faq.answer: Considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history, Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal (Shixian)'.

faq.question: How many of Li Bai's poems survive?
faq.answer: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive.

faq.question: What are the main themes in Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: The main themes related to his poetic literature are Daoism and wine.

faq.question: Where was Li Bai from?
faq.answer: Currently, it is widely accepted that he was an Central Asian from Suyab of the Tang Dynasty.

faq.question: Did Li Bai receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because his father was a merchant, Li Bai did not receive formal education.

faq.question: When did Li Bai die?
faq.answer: Li Bai died in 762 at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province (February 6, Tenbun 6, lunar calendar).
Began working as a servant for Oda Nobunaga.
Married Nene, the adopted daughter of Asano Nagakatsu (Eiroku 4).

Played an active role in the capture of Mitsukuri Castle in Omi, and when Oda Nobunaga installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as Shogun, he managed political affairs in Kyoto alongside Akechi Mitsuhide and Niwa Nagahide (September, Eiroku 11, lunar calendar).
Joined the subjugation of Asakura Yoshikage of Echizen Province, and distinguished himself by commanding the rearguard at Kanegasaki (Genki 1).
After the annihilation of the Azai clan, he was granted three districts in Omi, took up his post as the lord of Nagahama Castle, and changed his surname to Hashiba (Tensho 1).
Returned without permission while being dispatched to rescue Shibata Katsuie, and subjugated Matsunaga Hisahide of Yamato Province (Battle of Shigisan Castle) (Tensho 4).
Subjugated Ukita Naoie, a daimyo of Bizen and Mimasaka Provinces (Tensho 7).
Attacked and received the surrender of Bessho Nagaharu, the lord of Miki Castle in Harima (Battle of Miki). Captured Arikoyama Castle of Yamana Akihiro of Tajima Province (Tensho 8).
Captured Tottori Castle (Battle of Tottori Castle). Placed Awaji Province under his control by capturing Iwaya Castle (Tensho 9).
Requested reinforcements from Oda Nobunaga during the water attack on Takamatsu Castle in Bitchu (Tensho 10).
Received the news that his lord Oda Nobunaga had committed suicide in the Honno-ji Incident (June 2, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Defeated Akechi Mitsuhide at the Battle of Yamazaki (June 13, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
At the Kiyosu Conference, Oda Nobunaga's legitimate grandson Sanboshi was decided as the successor (June 27, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Held Oda Nobunaga's funeral with his adopted son Hashiba Hidekatsu as the chief mourner (October 15, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Conflict with Shibata Katsuie intensified, leading him to besiege Nagahama Castle, capture Kajiki Castle, and receive Sanboshi from Oda Nobutaka (Tensho 11).
Defeated Shibata Katsuie at the Battle of Shizugatake (April 21, Tensho 11, lunar calendar).
Began the construction of Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji (Tensho 11).
Conflicted with Oda Nobukatsu, and Tokugawa Ieyasu sided with Nobukatsu (Battle of Komaki and Nagakute) (Tensho 12).
Ikeda Tsuneoki, his son Motosuke, and Mori Nagayoshi were killed in action at the Battle of Nagakute (April 9, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Made peace with Oda Nobukatsu (November 11, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Appointed to the court rank of Senior Second Rank and the office of Palace Minister (Naidaijin) (March 10, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Kishu (March 21, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Shikoku (July 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Kampaku (Imperial Regent) (July 11, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Sassa Narimasa of Etchu surrendered to Hideyoshi (August 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Forced his younger sister Asahi to divorce and married her off to Ieyasu, and sent his mother Omandokoro as a hostage to Ieyasu (Tensho 14).
Was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court (September 9, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Daijo-daijin (Chancellor of the Realm) and established his regime (December 25, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Fought and was defeated by the Shimazu army led by Shimazu Iehisa at the Hetsugi River in Bungo (Battle of Hetsugigawa) (Tensho 14).
Led a large army of 200,000 to conquer Kyushu, resulting in the surrender of the Shimazu clan (Kyushu Campaign) (Tensho 15).
Issued the Bateren Edict expelling Christian missionaries (June, Tensho 15).
Implemented the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) (Tensho 16).
Tsurumatsu was born to his concubine Yodo-dono and was designated as his successor (Tensho 17).
Led a large army of 210,000 to dispatch troops to Kanto to suppress the Go-Hojo clan, and besieged Odawara Castle (Siege of Odawara) (Tensho 18).

After the death from illness of Tsurumatsu, whom he had designated as his successor, he adopted his nephew Hidetsugu, transferred the position of Kampaku to him, and was himself called Taiko (Tensho 19).
Ordered the tea master Sen no Rikyu to commit seppuku (Tensho 19).
Suppressed the rebellion led by Kunohe Masazane in the Tohoku region (Tensho 19).
The Imjin War (Japanese invasions of Korea) began (Bunroku 1).
Peace negotiations with Ming China began. His concubine Yodo-dono gave birth to Hideyori (Bunroku 2).
Disinherited Kampaku Toyotomi Hidetsugu, exiled him to Mount Koya, and later forced him to commit seppuku on charges of treason (Bunroku 4).
Peace negotiations with Ming China broke down (Bunroku 5).
Re-invaded Joseon Korea with an army of 140,000 (Jeongyu War) (Keicho 2).
Died at Fushimi Castle (August 18, Keicho 3, lunar calendar).

keyAchievements.title: Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku Period
keyAchievements.description: Along with Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu, he is known as one of the Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Succeeding Oda Nobunaga's Enterprise
keyAchievements.description: After the Honno-ji Incident, he defeated Akechi Mitsuhide and succeeded Oda Nobunaga's enterprise.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Japan
keyAchievements.description: Unified Japan by subduing the Go-Hojo clan and brought an end to the long-lasting Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Kampaku and Daijo-daijin
keyAchievements.description: Established his regime by being appointed Kampaku and Daijo-daijin, and was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court.

keyAchievements.title: Taiko Land Survey and Sword Hunt
keyAchievements.description: Stabilized the domestic situation in Japan by implementing the Taiko Land Survey (Taikou Kenchi) and the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) policies.

keyAchievements.title: Rise from the Lowest Class to Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Ascended to become the ruler of Japan despite being a person from the lowest social class in the country.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Osaka Castle
keyAchievements.description: Constructed Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: He was the main instigator who led the Imjin War in the first year of Bunroku (1592) and invaded Joseon Korea.

faq.question: What was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's background?
faq.answer: He is known to have been born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province. Because of his very low social status, he was conscious of his humble origins, leading to suspicions that records from his time and later periods were often distorted or glorified.

faq.question: Why couldn't Toyotomi Hideyoshi become a Shogun?
faq.answer: Coming from a commoner background and not being of the Minamoto bloodline, he could not become a Shogun due to the rule that 'only a Minamoto can become a Shogun'. Instead, he rose to the positions of Kampaku and Daijo-daijin.

faq.question: What were the main reasons Toyotomi Hideyoshi started the Imjin War?
