Published his first play, 'Catiline', under the pseudonym 'Brynjolf Bjarme', and had his first stage production, 'The Burial Mound'.
Became the artistic director of the Christiania Theater and married Suzannah Thoresen.
Son Sigurd was born.
Left Christiania for Sorrento, Italy, beginning 27 years of living abroad.
Published the play 'Brand', which brought him critical acclaim and financial success.
Published the play 'Peer Gynt', for which Edvard Grieg composed the incidental music.
Moved from Italy to Dresden, Germany.
Published his only collection of poetry, 'Poems' (Digte).
Wrote 'Emperor and Galilean', dramatizing the life of the Roman Emperor Julian the Apostate, and was knighted.
Moved to Munich and began work on his first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society'.
His first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society', was published and first performed.
Published the play 'A Doll's House', which brought him international recognition.
Published the play 'Ghosts', containing commentary on the morality of Ibsen's society.
Published the play 'An Enemy of the People', addressing controversial themes.
Published the play 'The Wild Duck', considered by many to be Ibsen's finest work.
Published the later work 'Hedda Gabler', exploring psychological conflict.
Returned to Christiania, Norway, after 27 years of living abroad.
Published the later work 'The Master Builder', exploring psychological conflict, and was made a Commander.
Received the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Olav.
Suffered several strokes.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Died at his home in Christiania at the age of 78.
The 'Ibsen Year' was celebrated in Norway and many other countries to commemorate the 100th anniversary of his death, and 'A Doll's House' was the world's most performed play.
The 'Delhi Ibsen Festival' began to be held annually in Delhi, India.
Håkon Anton Fagerås created two bronze busts of Ibsen to be installed in Ibsen Park in Sorrento, Italy, and in the Skien municipality.
A marble statue of Ibsen was created for the Ibsen Museum in Oslo.
Google featured a Doodle celebrating Henrik Ibsen's 185th birthday.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Modern Drama
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and referred to as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered Realistic Theater
keyAchievements.description: Though he also wrote lyrical epic works, he pioneered realistic theater.

keyAchievements.title: World's Most Performed Playwright
keyAchievements.description: He is the most frequently performed playwright in the world after Shakespeare.

keyAchievements.title: International Recognition
keyAchievements.description: Achieved international recognition with works such as 'Brand', 'Peer Gynt', and 'A Doll's House'.

keyAchievements.title: Redefined the Rules of Drama
keyAchievements.description: Completely rewrote the rules of drama through realism.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Influenced figures such as George Bernard Shaw, Oscar Wilde, and James Joyce.

faq.question: When was Henrik Ibsen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born on March 20, 1828, and died on May 23, 1906.

faq.question: How is Henrik Ibsen evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: He is considered the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and is known as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

faq.question: What are Henrik Ibsen's representative works?
faq.answer: His works include 'A Doll's House', 'Ghosts', 'An Enemy of the People', 'The Wild Duck', and 'Peer Gynt'.

faq.question: Did Henrik Ibsen win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: He was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1902, 1903, and 1904.

faq.question: What were Henrik Ibsen's political views?
faq.answer: He expressed anarchistic views, stating that the state is a curse upon the individual and should be abolished.

timeline.event: Born into a noble Prussian family in Berlin.
Began his education after his father died.
Educated by a tutor alongside his older brother.
Graduated from the Freiberg School of Mines and became a civil servant in the Prussian Department of Mines.
Toured rock salt mining regions in various Central European countries.
Inherited a large fortune upon his mother's death.
Began his expedition to Spanish America with the support of the Spanish Prime Minister.
Returned from South America.
Published 23 volumes of 'Personal Narrative of Travels to the Equinoctial Regions of the New Continent'.
Published 'Views of Nature' (Ansichten der Natur) in Berlin.
Demonstrated the idea and method of comparing climatic conditions across different countries through the 'Creation of Isothermal Lines'.
Taught physical geography at the University of Vienna.
Died at the age of 90 and was honored with a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Kosmos'
keyAchievements.description: After observing physical geography and ecology during his expeditions to South America and Central Asia, he compiled vast amounts of data to write 'Kosmos', a masterpiece regarded as a monumental work in modern geography.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Geography
keyAchievements.description: Became a founder of modern geography along with Carl Ritter.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Humboldt Current
keyAchievements.description: The ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru was named the Humboldt Current in honor of his investigations.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundations for Physical Geography and Geophysics
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundations of physical geography and geophysics through his investigations of the mountain ranges of tropical America.

keyAchievements.title: Observation and Study of Meteor Showers
keyAchievements.description: Conducted observations of meteor showers from Tenerife in the Canary Islands; his research on their periodicity became a foundation for modern astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Views of Nature'
keyAchievements.description: Investigated and studied Mount Vesuvius in Italy and published 'Views of Nature' in Berlin in 1807.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Isothermal Lines
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated the method of comparing climatic conditions of various countries in 'Des Lignes Isothermes' (1817) and discovered the rate of temperature decrease with increasing altitude.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Earth's Magnetism
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the strength of the Earth's magnetic field decreases from the poles toward the equator.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of International Magnetic Observatories and Meteorological Institute
keyAchievements.description: Created an international network of magnetic observatories and established a meteorological institute in Prussia.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Specimen Collection and Research
keyAchievements.description: Investigated the relationship between plants and their environment, collecting a massive herbarium of 60,000 specimens, which included thousands of new species and genera.

faq.question: Who was Alexander von Humboldt?
faq.answer: He was a German geographer, natural scientist, naturalist, and explorer, who along with Carl Ritter, became a founder of modern geography.

faq.question: What is his representative work?
faq.answer: His magnum opus is 'Kosmos', based on data from his expeditions in South America and Central Asia, which is regarded as a monumental achievement in modern geography.

faq.question: How is the Humboldt Current related to him?
faq.answer: The Humboldt Current was named in his honor to commemorate his investigation of the ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru.

faq.question: In what fields did he leave a legacy?
faq.answer: He made tremendous contributions to a variety of fields including flora and fauna, astronomy, climate, mineralogy, and oceanography, in addition to geography, natural science, natural history, and exploration.

timeline.event: Born as the eldest son of a Danish noble family.
Became interested in astronomy after observing a total solar eclipse and enrolled at the University of Copenhagen.
Realized the inaccuracy of the cross-staff and created a table of instrument corrections.
Returned to Copenhagen due to the war between Sweden and Denmark.
Studied at the University of Wittenberg and the University of Rostock, and graduated.
Lost part of his nose in a duel with his cousin Manderup Parsberg.
Became famous in astrology for predicting the death of the Ottoman Empire's Sultan.
Received a promise from Frederick II to be appointed as a canon of Roskilde Cathedral.
Poured his passion into astronomical observations while staying in Augsburg.
Discovered a new star (nova) in the constellation Cassiopeia and recorded observations for 14 months.
Married his lover, Kirsten.
Published the book 'De nova stella' (On the New Star).
His improved observation equipment in Augsburg was destroyed by a storm.
Stayed at the Hesse-Kassel observatory to meet William IV.
Took up the position of canon and established the Uraniborg observatory on the island of Hven.
Discovered a comet and recorded its observations.
Built the additional observatory, Stjerneborg.
Published the book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Progymnasmata' (Introduction to the New Astronomy).
His position became precarious following the death of Frederick II.
Published the book 'De Mundi Aetherei Recentioribus Phaenomenis'.
Experienced friction with the royal court after Christian IV's accession led to pension cuts, prompting him to leave the island of Hven.
Published the book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Mechanica'.
Moved to Prague at the invitation of Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II.
Died.
The book 'Astronomiae Instauratae Progymnasmata' was published (posthumously).

keyAchievements.title: Discovery and Observation of Supernova
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a nova in the constellation Cassiopeia in 1572 and kept observational records for 14 months.

keyAchievements.title: Precise Astronomical Observational Records
keyAchievements.description: Left behind massive and accurate astronomical records, achieving the most precise naked-eye observations without a telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Tychonic System
keyAchievements.description: Proposed his own 'Tychonic system' (a modified geocentric model), combining the Copernican and Ptolemaic systems.

keyAchievements.title: Proof of the Comet Phenomenon
keyAchievements.description: Proved that comet phenomena occur further away than the moon, disproving the prevailing geocentric theories of the time.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Observatories
keyAchievements.description: Built the Uraniborg and Stjerneborg observatories on the island of Hven, utilizing them as centers for astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Lunar Libration
keyAchievements.description: Observed the variation in the moon's longitude and discovered lunar libration, an oscillation of the moon's axis of rotation.

keyAchievements.title: Foundation for Kepler's Laws
keyAchievements.description: His precise observational data became the crucial foundation for his assistant Johannes Kepler to discover the laws of planetary motion.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Medicine and Pedagogy
keyAchievements.description: Advanced the study of herbalism, impacting medical progress, and contributed to education in astronomy and herbalism through the Uraniborg research institute.

faq.question: Why did Tycho Brahe lose his nose?
faq.answer: A portion of his nose was severed by a sword during a duel with his cousin Manderup Parsberg in 1566.

faq.question: How did Tycho Brahe die?
faq.answer: He is believed to have died from a ruptured bladder in 1601 after holding his urine out of politeness following heavy drinking at a banquet.

faq.question: What cosmological model did Tycho Brahe propose?
faq.answer: He proposed the unique 'Tychonic system', in which the Earth is fixed at the center of the universe, the Sun and Moon revolve around the Earth, and the other planets revolve around the Sun.

faq.question: Did Tycho Brahe use a telescope?
faq.answer: No, he conducted the most precise observations possible without using a telescope.

faq.question: What impact did Tycho Brahe's observational data have?
faq.answer: His extensive and precise observational data served as the decisive foundation for his student Johannes Kepler to discover Kepler's laws of planetary motion.

timeline.event: Born near Farab in Central Asia.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and centered around Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Left Numerous Writings
keyAchievements.description: Left numerous writings, including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

keyAchievements.title: Multidisciplinary Scholar
keyAchievements.description: Specialized in science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

keyAchievements.title: First Iranian Philosopher of the Islamic Era
keyAchievements.description: He is the first Iranian philosopher to emerge in the Islamic era.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as the Second Teacher
keyAchievements.description: Revered as the 'Second Teacher' following Aristotle.

faq.question: Who was Al-Farabi?
faq.answer: He was an Islamic (Turkic) philosopher active from 872 to 950 who specialized in various fields including science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

faq.question: What are Al-Farabi's major achievements?
faq.answer: He established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and Neoplatonism, and left behind numerous works including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

faq.question: What was Al-Farabi's epithet?
faq.answer: He was revered as the 'Second Teacher', following Aristotle.

faq.question: Where was Al-Farabi born?
faq.answer: He was born near Farab in Central Asia.

faq.question: Which languages was Al-Farabi fluent in?
faq.answer: He was proficient in dozens of languages, including Arabic, Persian, and Turkic.

timeline.event: Birth.
Moved to Boston at age 8, where his mother was.
Resigned from his professorship at Harvard University.
Settled in Rome.
Lived as a Catholic freethinker in a convent in Rome.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Unique Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to combine realism and idealism, establishing a unique philosophy which argued that while the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith', certain knowledge of 'essences' is possible.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Book
keyAchievements.description: Authored the novel 'The Last Puritan'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined Famous Aphorism
keyAchievements.description: Left behind the famous aphorism, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.'

faq.question: What was Santayana's main philosophy?
faq.answer: Santayana held a unique philosophy attempting to combine realism and idealism, asserting that the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith' but arguing for certain knowledge of 'essences'. His thought is characterized by an attempt to merge robust naturalism with beautiful romanticism.

faq.question: What is Santayana's famous aphorism?
faq.answer: Santayana left the famous quote, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.'

faq.question: What is Santayana's representative work?
faq.answer: Santayana's representative work is the novel 'The Last Puritan'.

timeline.event: Born in Gloucestershire.
Enrolled at the University of Oxford.
Received a Master of Arts degree.
Moved to Cambridge to study the Bible.
Completed an English translation of the New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts and had it printed in Germany.
Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg.
Wrote 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue', refuting Thomas More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.
Arrested in the Netherlands on charges of translating the Bible into English.
Sentenced to death by an imperial decree.
Condemned as a heretic and burned at the stake.

keyAchievements.title: English Translation of the Koine Greek Bible
keyAchievements.description: Influenced by John Wycliffe, he translated the Koine Greek Bible into English.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of New Words
keyAchievements.description: Created new words that did not previously exist for the purpose of English translation.

keyAchievements.title: Translation and Printing of the English New Testament Based on Original Hebrew and Greek Texts
keyAchievements.description: Completed the English New Testament in 1526 through translation work based on original Hebrew and Greek texts and had it printed in Germany.

keyAchievements.title: Translation of the Pentateuch
keyAchievements.description: Translated the Pentateuch in Hamburg in 1529.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a piece in 1531 refuting Thomas More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for the King James Bible
keyAchievements.description: Seventy percent of the English version of the King James Bible is based on Tyndale's Bible.

keyAchievements.title: Spoke Seven Languages
keyAchievements.description: Was able to speak seven languages including English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.

faq.question: When was William Tyndale born?
faq.answer: William Tyndale was born in Gloucestershire in 1494.

faq.question: What are William Tyndale's main achievements?
faq.answer: He translated the Koine Greek Bible into English, completed and printed the English New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts. He also laid the foundation for the King James Bible.

faq.question: How did William Tyndale die?
faq.answer: He was arrested in 1535 on charges of translating the Bible into English and was burned at the stake as a heretic on October 6, 1536.

faq.question: What languages could William Tyndale speak?
faq.answer: He could speak seven languages: English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.

faq.question: What was William Tyndale's relationship with Thomas More?
faq.answer: In 1531, William Tyndale harshly criticized Thomas More by writing 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue', a rebuttal to More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.

timeline.event: His father, John Chaucer, was kidnapped by his aunt in an attempt to marry the 12-year-old boy to her daughter in order to keep property in Ipswich.
Born in London as the son of a wealthy wine merchant.
His father, John Chaucer, married Agnes Copton, who inherited properties, including 24 shops in London, from her uncle, Hamo de Copton.
First mentioned as a page to Elizabeth de Burgh, Countess of Ulster.
Traveled to France as a member of the English army with Elizabeth's husband, Lionel of Antwerp, during Edward III's invasion of France in the Hundred Years' War.
Captured and taken as a prisoner of war during the siege of Rheims, and released after Edward III paid a ransom of £16.
Married Philippa Roet.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Canterbury Tales'
keyAchievements.description: Best known as the author of the story collection 'The Canterbury Tales'.

keyAchievements.title: Called the 'Father of English literature' or 'Father of English poetry'
keyAchievements.description: Considered the first writer to create works in the vernacular English, rather than French or Latin.

faq.question: Who was Geoffrey Chaucer?
faq.answer: An English author, poet, bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat, best known as the author of the story collection 'The Canterbury Tales'.

faq.question: What is Geoffrey Chaucer's representative work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is the unfinished 'The Canterbury Tales', a story collection influenced by Giovanni Boccaccio's 'Decameron'.

faq.question: Is there sufficient information about Geoffrey Chaucer's early life?
faq.answer: The text states that there is very little documentary evidence remaining about Chaucer's early life and education.

timeline.event: Born in Aix-en-Provence.
Enrolled at the Collège Bourbon in Aix and became friends with Émile Zola.
Enrolled in the free municipal drawing school in Aix to study painting.
Entered the law school at the University of Aix-en-Provence but immersed himself in drawing practice.
Moved to Paris for his artistic development but failed the entrance exam to the École des Beaux-Arts.
His paintings were exhibited at the 'Salon des Refusés' established by Napoleon III.
His son Paul was born to Hortense Fiquet.
Participated in the first group exhibition of the 'Anonymous Society of Painters, Sculptors, Engravers, etc.'.
Submitted 16 works to the third Impressionist exhibition.
With the help of Antoine Guillemet, exhibited 'Portrait of M. L. A.' at the Salon, marking his first and last Salon exhibition.
Married Hortense Fiquet.
His father died, and he inherited a fortune, achieving financial stability.
Converted to Catholicism.
Ambroise Vollard held Cézanne's first solo exhibition at his Paris gallery, leading to a re-evaluation of his work.
Death of his mother.
Built his own studio in Aix-en-Provence and moved there in 1903.
Exhibited for the first time at the newly established Salon d'Automne.
An entire room at the Salon d'Automne was dedicated to Cézanne's works.
Died of pneumonia at the age of 67.
Two posthumous retrospective exhibitions (a watercolor exhibition at Galerie Bernheim-Jeune and a retrospective at the Salon d'Automne) inspired many artists.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: A French Post-Impressionist painter who served as a bridge between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a New Painting Language
keyAchievements.description: Created a new painting language by altering traditional perspective and emphasizing the fundamental structure and formal characteristics of objects.

keyAchievements.title: Color Plane and Brushstroke Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Characterized by unique and exploratory brushwork, using color planes and small brushstrokes to form complex fields.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Geometric Forms
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the pictorial idea of treating nature in terms of simple geometric shapes like the cylinder, sphere, and cone, and placing objects within perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Expression of Depth through Binocular Vision
keyAchievements.description: Explored binocular vision to express visual perception of the same phenomenon, providing a new aesthetic experience of depth.

keyAchievements.title: Montagne Sainte-Victoire Series
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated precise observation of nature and pictorial exploration through his series on Montagne Sainte-Victoire in his hometown of Aix-en-Provence.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: Provided decisive insight and inspiration to early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, including Picasso and Matisse, earning him the title 'the father of us all'.

faq.question: What kind of painter was Paul Cézanne?
faq.answer: He was a French Post-Impressionist painter who bridged late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

faq.question: How were Cézanne's works received during his lifetime?
faq.answer: His paintings were initially misunderstood and ridiculed by contemporary art critics, but they were re-evaluated after his first solo exhibition in 1895, and in his later years, he was revered as a master by younger artists.

faq.question: What was Cézanne's impact on modern art?
faq.answer: He introduced new modes of expression that influenced early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, notably providing decisive inspiration to Cubist and Fauvist painters like Picasso and Matisse.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Cézanne's painting technique?
faq.answer: He altered traditional perspective, emphasized the fundamental structure and formal traits of objects, and pursued geometric simplification, treating nature as cylinders, spheres, and cones.

timeline.event: Born in Paris, France.
His father, François Baudelaire, died when he was 6 years old.
Sent to Kolkata, India, per his stepfather's wishes, but only traveled as far as Réunion Island before returning to France.
Known as a dandy in artistic circles, though he had not yet published any writings.
Squandered half of the inheritance from his biological father and asked his mother for money to achieve success as a writer.
Published his first art critique, 'Salon of 1845'.
Wrote his second Salon review, gaining a reputation as an advocate and critic of Romanticism.
Published the novella 'La Fanfarlo'.
Participated in the revolution.
Suffered from deteriorating health, heavy debts, and irregular creative output.
Published his first poetry collection and most famous work, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil).
Attempted to leave Paris and settle in Honfleur, but it did not go well.
Continued his writing activities, translating Edgar Allan Poe's 'The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket' and writing critiques.
Left Jeanne Duval when another of her lovers moved in with them.
Published another edition of 'Les Fleurs du mal' with 6 poems removed but a substantial number of new poems added.
Attempted to run for the Académie Française but withdrew due to criticism from critic Charles Augustin Sainte-Beuve.
Traveled to Brussels to publish books and give lectures due to his debts, but the publishing industry was indifferent.
Died in Paris in August.
On May 11, the 6 censored poems from 'Les Fleurs du mal' were acquitted and republished in France.

keyAchievements.title: Activity as a Critic and Poet
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the literary world by working as a French critic and poet.

keyAchievements.title: Won Literature Competitions
keyAchievements.description: Showed early literary talent by winning an honorable mention in Latin poetry and 2nd prize in French poetry at national competitions.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Salon of 1845'
keyAchievements.description: Published his first art critique, 'Salon of 1845', gaining attention for its audacity.

keyAchievements.title: Published the Novella 'La Fanfarlo'
keyAchievements.description: Continued his career as a writer by publishing the novella 'La Fanfarlo'.

keyAchievements.title: Translated the Works of Edgar Allan Poe
keyAchievements.description: Translated Edgar Allan Poe's works and introduced them to French readers.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Les Fleurs du mal'
keyAchievements.description: Published his first poetry collection and most famous work, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil), in 1857.

keyAchievements.title: Late Career Works
keyAchievements.description: Published works such as the prose poems to be included in 'Le Spleen de Paris', 'L'Œuvre et la vie d'Eugène Delacroix', and 'Le Peintre de la vie moderne'.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as the Father of Symbolism
keyAchievements.description: Praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine, he was revered as the father of Symbolism.

faq.question: What was Charles Baudelaire's profession?
faq.answer: He was a French critic and poet.

faq.question: What is Charles Baudelaire's most famous work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is his first poetry collection, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil), published in 1857.

faq.question: What controversy surrounded the publication of 'Les Fleurs du mal'?
faq.answer: 'Les Fleurs du mal' was prosecuted for an offense against public morals for being indecent, resulting in a fine, but it was acquitted in 1949.

faq.question: Which author's works did Charles Baudelaire translate?
faq.answer: He translated the works of Edgar Allan Poe, whose short stories had a profound influence on him.

faq.question: What artistic movement did Charles Baudelaire influence?
faq.answer: He was praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine, and revered as the father of Symbolism.

timeline.event: Born in Paris on June 7
Moved to Peru with his family
Left Peru after his guardian fell from power and moved to Orléans, France
Entered the naval preparatory school and served as a pilot and in the navy
Returned to Paris and got a job at a stock brokerage
Married Danish woman Mette-Sophie Gad and started painting as a hobby
Moved to a house with a studio in the Vaugirard district
Submitted works to the Impressionist exhibition
Quit his career as a stockbroker due to the financial crisis and turned into a full-time painter
In October, asked Camille Pissarro for help as a full-time painter
Moved to Rouen with his family, and in November, the whole family moved to Copenhagen
In June, returned to Paris with his son Clovis
In May, submitted 19 paintings and 1 wood relief to the 8th Impressionist Exhibition
Traveled to Panama and Martinique with Charles Laval
In October, visited Arles and lived with Vincent van Gogh, and returned to Paris in late December
Invited to Les XX (The Twenty) exhibition
In February, raised funds through the Hôtel Drouot auction, sailed to Tahiti on April 1, and married Teha'amana in November
At the end of the year, exhibited Tahitian works at the Goupil Gallery
Left Tahiti on June 14, returned to France in August, and held the Durand-Ruel exhibition in November
In early year, prepared woodcuts for the travelogue 'Noa Noa' and separated from his wife Mette
In February, attempted the Hôtel Drouot auction but failed, submitted the ceramic sculpture Oviri to the Salon in April, departed for Tahiti again on June 28, and arrived in September
In April, heard the news of his daughter Aline's death and suffered from poor health and financial difficulties
In November, held an exhibition at the Vollard Gallery
Signed a supply contract with art dealer Ambroise Vollard
In February, became the editor of the local newspaper 'Les Guêpes'
In September, resigned as editor of 'Les Guêpes' and moved to Atuona on Hiva Oa island in the Marquesas Islands
Died on May 8 in the Marquesas Islands

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Post-Impressionism and Symbolism Movements
keyAchievements.description: As a French painter, sculptor, printmaker, ceramist, and writer, he made significant contributions to the Post-Impressionism and Symbolism movements.

keyAchievements.title: Practice of Wood Engraving and Woodcut
keyAchievements.description: He was an influential practitioner of wood engraving and woodcut as art forms.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Synthetism Style
keyAchievements.description: He was recognized by later generations for his experimental use of color and Synthetist style, distinct from Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Representative Cloisonnism Work 'The Yellow Christ'
keyAchievements.description: He created 'The Yellow Christ', a representative work of Cloisonnism, simplified with bold black outlines and areas of pure color.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Primitivism
keyAchievements.description: He was the first artist to systematically use and achieve popular success with Primitivism, characterized by exaggerated body proportions, animal totems, geometric designs, and stark contrasts.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His bold, colorful, and design-oriented paintings inspired many modern artists, including Vincent van Gogh, Henri Matisse, and Pablo Picasso.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Masterpiece 'Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?'
keyAchievements.description: He completed the massive painting 'Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?', considered his masterpiece and artistic testament.

faq.question: What art movements is Paul Gauguin associated with?
faq.answer: He is primarily associated with the Post-Impressionism and Symbolism movements and is regarded as a pioneer of the Primitivist style.

faq.question: What is the typical style of Paul Gauguin's works?
faq.answer: His works feature experimental colors and a Synthetist style, using bold outlines and flat areas of color under the influence of Cloisonnism.

faq.question: What was Paul Gauguin's relationship with Vincent van Gogh?
faq.answer: In 1888, he painted with Vincent van Gogh in Arles for 9 weeks, but their relationship deteriorated, and they parted ways after the incident where Vincent cut off his own ear.

faq.question: Why did Paul Gauguin leave for Tahiti?
faq.answer: He left for Tahiti to escape European civilization and 'everything that is artificial and conventional' to find new artistic inspiration.

faq.question: Was Paul Gauguin a successful painter during his lifetime?
faq.answer: He did not achieve great success during his lifetime, but after his death, he gained popularity through the efforts of art dealer Ambroise Vollard and others, and had a profound influence on modern art.

timeline.event: Birth of English painter and poet William Blake (November 28)
Became an apprentice to an engraver at age 14
Married at age 25
Published 'Poetical Sketches'
Published 'Songs of Innocence'
Published the illuminated book 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell'
Published the illuminated book 'Songs of Experience'

Attempted 100 illustrations for Dante's 'Divine Comedy' but left it unfinished
Death of William Blake (August 12)

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Poet and Painter
keyAchievements.description: He was active as a major British artist, presenting poetry and paintings intertwined with mystery and fantasy.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Original Illuminated Books
keyAchievements.description: He published his own unique illuminated books by inventing relief etching, placing illustrations on every page.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Works
keyAchievements.description: He left behind several famous illuminated books, including 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell' (1790), 'Songs of Experience' (1794), and 'Illustrations of the Book of Job' (1825).

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Artistic Status of Illustration
keyAchievements.description: He is evaluated as the figure who elevated illustration to a level comparable to other paintings.

faq.question: What was William Blake's profession?
faq.answer: He was an English painter and poet.

faq.question: What kind of painting did William Blake despise?
faq.answer: He despised paintings that merely copied the external appearance of nature, such as portraits or landscapes.

faq.question: What was William Blake's preferred medium for painting?
faq.answer: He avoided oil painting and considered watercolor to be the highest form of expression.

faq.question: What are William Blake's representative illuminated books?
faq.answer: His representative illuminated books include 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell' (1790), 'Songs of Experience' (1794), and 'Illustrations of the Book of Job' (1825).

faq.question: How did William Blake produce his illuminated books?
faq.answer: He published illuminated books by placing illustrations on every page and inventing his own method of relief etching.

Published the second booklet of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'

Published the third booklet of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'

The Collin family grave was moved to another cemetery where younger Collin family members were buried

keyAchievements.title: Fame as a Fairy Tale Writer
keyAchievements.description: He was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, but is best known for his fairy tales.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Writing and Translation
keyAchievements.description: His fairy tales consist of 156 stories across 9 volumes and have been translated into more than 125 languages.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Influence
keyAchievements.description: His fairy tales are deeply ingrained in the collective consciousness of Western culture.

keyAchievements.title: Success of 'A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager'
keyAchievements.description: He achieved considerable success in 1829 with the short story 'A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'The Improvisatore'
keyAchievements.description: His first novel, 'The Improvisatore', published in 1835, received immediate acclaim.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.'
keyAchievements.description: He first attempted the fairy tale genre with this collection, published in installments between 1835 and 1837.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing International Fame with 'The Little Mermaid'
keyAchievements.description: 'The Little Mermaid', included in the third booklet, established Andersen's international fame.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Travelogues
keyAchievements.description: He published several long travelogues, such as 'In Sweden' (1851) and 'A Poet's Bazaar', which received favorable reviews.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Picture Book Without Pictures'
keyAchievements.description: He achieved good results with 'Picture Book Without Pictures', published in 1840.

keyAchievements.title: International Acclaim
keyAchievements.description: He received acclaim across Europe, starting fairy tale series in 1838 and 1845.

faq.question: What kind of writer is Hans Christian Andersen best known as?
faq.answer: He was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, but is best known for his fairy tales.

faq.question: How many languages have Andersen's fairy tales been translated into?
faq.answer: His fairy tales consist of a total of 156 stories across 9 volumes and have been translated into more than 125 languages.

faq.question: What are Andersen's most famous fairy tales?
faq.answer: His most famous fairy tales include 'The Emperor's New Clothes', 'The Red Shoes', 'The Little Match Girl', 'The Ugly Duckling', 'The Little Mermaid', 'Thumbelina', and 'The Snow Queen'.

faq.question: When and where was Andersen born?
faq.answer: Andersen was born on April 2, 1805, in Odense, Denmark.

faq.question: Who were Andersen's parents?
faq.answer: His father received elementary education, and his mother was an illiterate washerwoman.

faq.question: Did Andersen ever meet Charles Dickens?
faq.answer: Yes, Andersen first met Charles Dickens during his visit to England in June 1847, and visited England again in 1857 to meet Dickens.

faq.question: When did Andersen die?
faq.answer: Andersen died on August 4, 1875, at the age of 70, at Rolighed, a villa near Copenhagen.

faq.question: How was his childhood reflected in Andersen's works?
faq.answer: 'The Little Match Girl' was based on his poor mother, 'The Snow Queen' on his father's death, and 'The Ugly Duckling' on the mistreatment he suffered due to his background.

timeline.event: Born in Leipzig as Clara Josephine Wieck.
Her parents divorced.
Made her official debut at the Gewandhaus in Leipzig.
Met Robert Schumann.
Composed Four Polonaises for Piano, Op. 1.
Toured Paris and other European cities with her father.
Performed her Piano Concerto in A minor with the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra conducted by Mendelssohn.
Accepted Robert Schumann's marriage proposal.
Appointed 'Royal and Imperial Austrian Chamber Virtuoso', Austria's highest musical honor.
Married the composer Robert Schumann.
Collaborated with Robert Schumann to publish a work based on Friedrich Rückert's poem cycle 'Liebesfrühling' (Spring of Love).
Met violinist Joseph Joachim for the first time.
Premiered Robert Schumann's Piano Concerto in Dresden.
Composed Piano Trio in G minor, Op. 17.
Rescued her children during the May Uprising in Dresden.
Met Johannes Brahms.
Composed Three Romances for Violin and Piano, Op. 22.
Robert Schumann suffered a mental breakdown, attempted suicide, and was admitted to an asylum.
Toured England for the first time.
Her husband Robert Schumann died.
Gave the first performance of Brahms' piano solo work, Variations and Fugue on a Theme by Handel.
Continued her sixteen annual winter-spring tours of England.
Worked on arrangements of 41 of Robert Schumann's lieder commissioned by a publisher.
Appointed as the first piano teacher at the newly founded Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt.
Performed Beethoven's Choral Fantasy with the newly founded Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra.
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's letters (Jugendbriefe).
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's piano works by adding fingerings and other instructions.
Gave her last performance in Frankfurt.
Maintained her teaching position.
Passed away at the age of 76.
A portrait created by Andreas Staub in 1835 was featured on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote.

keyAchievements.title: Renowned Concert Pianist
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era, she exerted her influence through a 61-year concert career.

keyAchievements.title: Innovator of Piano Recital Format
keyAchievements.description: She changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works, and made playing from memory the standard for concerts.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Composer
keyAchievements.description: She composed works in various genres, including piano solo pieces, piano concertos, chamber music, choral works, and lieder.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Piano Educator
keyAchievements.description: From 1878, she worked as an influential piano educator at Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt, attracting international students.

keyAchievements.title: Editor of Robert Schumann's Works
keyAchievements.description: She edited the publications of her husband Robert Schumann's works, and added instructions to his letters and piano works.

keyAchievements.title: Promoter of Robert Schumann's Music
keyAchievements.description: Throughout her life, she tirelessly promoted her husband Robert Schumann's works to ensure they were recognized and appreciated.

faq.question: Who was Clara Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann was a German pianist, composer, and piano teacher, evaluated as one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann marry Robert Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann married the composer Robert Schumann on September 12, 1840.

faq.question: How did Clara Schumann influence piano performance?
faq.answer: She made playing from memory the standard for concerts, and changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann die?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann died on May 20, 1896, at the age of 76, and was buried next to her husband Robert Schumann in Bonn.

faq.question: Is there a banknote featuring Clara Schumann's portrait?
faq.answer: Yes, Clara Schumann's portrait, created by Andreas Staub in 1835, was used on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote from 1989 to 2002.

timeline.event: Born in Bristol, Gloucestershire, England.
Entire family immigrated to New York, USA.
Family settled in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Admitted to Geneva Medical College in New York state.
Graduated top of her class from Geneva Medical College.
Dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers upon the outbreak of the American Civil War.

Founded a medical college in London.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Graduated Top of Her Class from Geneva Medical College
keyAchievements.description: In 1849, she graduated at the top of her class from Geneva Medical College in New York, becoming a doctor.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Medical Colleges
keyAchievements.description: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Medical Activities During the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: When the American Civil War broke out in 1861, she dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers.

faq.question: Who was Elizabeth Blackwell?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Blackwell was an English physician born in 1821 and died in 1910.

faq.question: When did Elizabeth Blackwell decide to become a doctor?
faq.answer: She decided to become a doctor in Cincinnati, Ohio, under the influence of Harriet Beecher Stowe.

faq.question: What kind of education did Elizabeth Blackwell receive?
faq.answer: She entered Geneva Medical College in New York state in 1847 and graduated at the top of her class in 1849.

faq.question: What medical institutions did Elizabeth Blackwell establish?
faq.answer: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

timeline.event: Dispatched to China with Yun Sa-ung, Choi Cheon-gu, and others to learn the designs of astronomical instruments.

Freed from his status as a government slave in recognition of his contributions to manufacturing astronomical instruments, and appointed as an official of the 5th junior rank at the Royal Bureau of Attire.
Promoted to the 5th senior rank and ordered to manufacture the Gaengjeomjigi, the first water clock.
Granted the military rank of Hogun (4th senior rank).
Completed the Jagyeokru, an automatic water clock powered by water.
Participated in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type, under the general direction of Yi Cheon.
Completed the Ongnu, an astronomical clock, and installed it in the Heumgyeonggak pavilion.

keyAchievements.title: Jagyeokru
keyAchievements.description: He completed the Jagyeokru, a hydro-powered automatic water clock that strikes the time.

keyAchievements.title: Ongnu
keyAchievements.description: He manufactured the Ongnu, an astronomical clock that displays the movement of the sun and moon as models, allowing observation of time, seasons, and celestial movements.

keyAchievements.title: Astronomical Instruments
keyAchievements.description: He produced various astronomical observation instruments such as the Daeganui, Soganui, Hyeonju-ilgu, Cheonpyeong-ilgu, Jeongnam-ilgu, Angbu-ilgu, Ilseongjeongsiui, and Gyupyo.

keyAchievements.title: Gabinja
keyAchievements.description: He contributed to the advancement of printing technology by participating in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type made of copper.

faq.question: What is Jang Yeong-sil's birth year?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil's birth year is not clearly recorded, but it is estimated that he was born between 1389 and 1390, judging from records indicating he was about 34 years old when he was freed from slavery in 1423.

faq.question: What was Jang Yeong-sil's social status?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil was originally a government slave of Dongnae-hyeon, but his outstanding technical skills were recognized, and he was promoted by King Taejong and King Sejong.

faq.question: Why did Jang Yeong-sil step down from his official position?
faq.answer: Due to an incident where King Sejong's palanquin broke, he was accused of disrespect to the King, faced dismissal from office, and subsequently disappeared from history.

faq.question: What were Jang Yeong-sil's family relations?
faq.answer: His father was an immigrant from the Yuan dynasty, originally from Suzhou/Hangzhou, and his mother was a gisaeng from Dongnae-hyeon, Joseon. He had one younger sister, and his younger female cousin is said to have married the astronomer Kim Dam.

Entered Myeongdong Elementary School and published the literary magazine 'New Myeongdong'
Graduated from Myeongdong Elementary School and transferred to Hwaryong County First Elementary School

Transferred to Gwangmyeong Middle School after the closure of Soongsil Middle School
Graduated from Gwangmyeong Middle School

Changed his name to 'Hiranuma' under the Japanese name-changing policy
Graduated from the Department of Literature at Yonhi Technical College

Transferred to the Department of English Literature at Doshisha University in Kyoto

Sentenced to 2 years in prison by the Kyoto District Court and imprisoned in Fukuoka Prison
Died in prison at Fukuoka Prison
His posthumous works were first introduced in the Kyunghyang Shinmun and a memorial service was held

His posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' was officially published
The 'Yun Dong-ju Monument' engraved with his posthumous work 'Prologue' was erected at Yonsei University
The Yun Dong-ju Literature Award was established and the first recipient was selected
